blocks.text: Imagine your brain while playing a video game. You're keeping track of your score, how many lives you have left, and your character's name all at the same time. A variable is how a program does the same thing. It's a named container that holds a piece of information so the program can remember it and use it later.

blocks.alt: A box labeled 'score' containing the value 0, showing a variable as a named container
blocks.caption: A variable is just a named container the name stays the same, the value inside can change.

blocks.text: Without variables, every time your program runs it forgets everything the score resets, the name disappears, the progress is gone. Variables give your program memory.
To create a variable in JavaScript you use the word 'let', followed by the name you want to give it, then an equals sign, then the value you want to store. Every part has a job.

blocks.alt: The line 'let score = 0' broken into three labeled parts: the keyword let in purple, the name score in blue, and the value 0 in green. A second example shows let playerName = 'Jordan' with the string highlighted in amber.
blocks.caption: Every variable declaration has three parts: the keyword, the name, and the value.

blocks.label: The word 'let'
blocks.text: You only use 'let' the very first time you create a variable. It tells JavaScript: make a new container with this name. After that, you just use the name by itself.
Variables can hold different kinds of values. Right now there are three kinds to know about.

blocks.desc: A number. No quotes. Used for anything you might do math with.
A string. Always wrapped in quotes. Used for words and text.
A boolean. Only ever true or false. No quotes. Used for yes/no situations.

blocks.label: Quotes matter
blocks.text: 42 is a number. '42' is a string that looks like a number but isn't. true is a boolean. 'true' is a string. The quotes make all the difference.

content.heading: Syntax the spelling rules of JavaScript

blocks.text: Every language has rules. JavaScript has syntax specific rules about how code must be written. Break them and JavaScript refuses to run. There are only five rules to know right now.

blocks.alt: Five syntax rules shown as annotated code examples: semicolons at line ends, case-sensitive names, quotes around text, no spaces in names, and let used only once per variable.
blocks.caption: When you see an error, check these five rules first one of them is almost always the cause.

blocks.text: Once a variable exists you can put a new value into it at any time. You don't use 'let' again just write the name, equals sign, and the new value. The old value is gone.

blocks.desc: No 'let' this time score already exists. This just replaces whatever was inside with 10.

blocks.alt: Two boxes showing score changing from 0 to 10, with an arrow between them
blocks.caption: Reassigning a variable replaces the old value completely it doesn't keep both.

blocks.label: The old value is gone
blocks.text: The box only ever holds one thing at a time. When you put a new value in, the old one disappears completely JavaScript does not keep a history.

One of the most common things you'll do is update a variable based on its own current value like adding points to a score, or subtracting a life. This looks a little strange at first but makes total sense once you read it the right way.

blocks.desc: Read the right side first: take what's in score, add 1 to it. Then store the result back into score. The right side always runs before the left side stores it.

lines: score = score + 5; // take 0, add 5 → score is now 5
score = score * 2; // take 5, multiply by 2 → score is now 10
score = score - 3; // take 10, subtract 3 → score is now 7

blocks.label: Read the right side first
blocks.text: score = score + 5 means: calculate score + 5 first, then store the answer back into score. The equals sign here means 'store this result', not 'these two things are equal'.

You can technically name a variable anything x, z, blah. JavaScript won't stop you. But in a week you'll open your code and have no idea what 'x' means. Good variable names make code readable to you, and to anyone else who reads it.

blocks.alt: Side by side comparison of good variable names like playerScore versus bad names like x
blocks.caption: Good variable names make your code readable. Bad ones make it a mystery even to you.

blocks.label: camelCase
blocks.text: When a variable name has multiple words, JavaScript developers smash them together and capitalize each new word: playerScore, firstName, isGameOver. This is called camelCase the capital letters look like camel humps.
A real program doesn't just have one variable it has many, all alive at the same time. Each one is its own container with its own name and value. They don't interfere with each other.

blocks.alt: Four variable boxes labeled score, name, lives, and isWinning sitting side by side
blocks.caption: A real program keeps many variables alive at the same time each in its own container.

exercises.title: What does 'let' do?
tasks: What does the word 'let' do in JavaScript?
exercises.hint: Think about what you're telling JavaScript to create.
exercises.explanation: 'let' tells JavaScript to create a new container with that name. You only use it the first time after that, just use the name by itself.

exercises.title: Spot the correct declaration
tasks: Which of these correctly declares a variable called lives and sets it to 3?
exercises.hint: A declaration needs let, a name, an equals sign, and a value. All four parts.
exercises.explanation: A variable declaration always follows this pattern: let, then the name, then =, then the value. All four parts are required.

exercises.title: Complete the variable
tasks: Drop the correct words into the blanks to declare a variable called score set to 10.
exercises.hint: Every variable needs let, a name, an equals sign, and a value.
exercises.explanation: Every variable starts with 'let' the first time, then the name, then = and the value.

exercises.title: Drop every part of the declaration
tasks: Declare a variable called lives and set it to 3.
exercises.hint: A variable declaration has four parts: keyword, name, equals sign, value.
exercises.explanation: let lives = 3; has four parts: the keyword let, the name lives, the equals sign =, and the value 3. All four are required.

exercises.title: Declare a different variable
tasks: Fill in the blanks to declare a variable called speed and set it to 5.
exercises.hint: Same pattern as before: let, name, =, value.
exercises.explanation: The pattern is always: let name = value; 'const' and 'var' are real JavaScript keywords but we use 'let' for now.

exercises.title: Type the keyword and value
tasks: Declare age to be 14.
exercises.hint: The keyword that creates a new variable is three letters. The value is a number, no quotes.
exercises.explanation: 'let' creates the variable. 14 is the value stored inside it. Both are required.

exercises.title: Sort the values by type
tasks: Drag each value into the correct bucket: String, Number, or Boolean.
exercises.hint: Text in quotes is a string. Plain numbers are numbers. true and false without quotes are booleans.
exercises.explanation: 'false' in quotes is a string it just looks like a boolean. Only true and false without quotes are real booleans.

exercises.title: What does this print?
tasks: Read the code carefully. What number does console.log print?
exercises.hint: Follow the code top to bottom. What is score equal to by line 3?
exercises.explanation: score starts at 5, then gets replaced by 20. The old value is gone. The console prints whatever is in the variable at that moment, which is 20.

exercises.title: Drop the reassignment
tasks: score already exists on line 1. Drop the right tokens to change it to 99 on line 2. No 'let' this time.
exercises.hint: You only use 'let' the very first time. After that just write the name.
exercises.explanation: Reassignment uses just the variable name with no 'let'. The name on the left, equals sign, new value on the right.

exercises.title: Put the code in order
tasks: Drag these lines into the correct order to make the code work.
exercises.hint: You have to create a variable before you can use it.
exercises.explanation: Variables must be declared with let before you can assign values or log them. Order always matters in JavaScript.

exercises.title: What does this print? (two steps)
tasks: Follow the code step by step. What does the console print?
exercises.hint: Read the right side of line 2 first: score + 5. What is score at that point?
exercises.explanation: score starts at 10. Then score + 5 = 15, and that gets stored back into score. So the console prints 15.

exercises.title: Drop the self-update
tasks: Fill in both blanks to add 3 to lives using lives itself.
exercises.hint: The variable name goes on both sides of the equals sign. Right side runs first.
exercises.explanation: lives = lives + 3 reads the current value of lives (2), adds 3, and stores the result (5) back. The variable name appears on both sides.

exercises.title: Type the full self-update
tasks: Add 5 to coins using coins itself.
exercises.hint: The variable name goes on the right side too. Read the right side first, then store the result.
exercises.explanation: coins = coins + 5 reads the current value (10), adds 5, and stores 15 back into coins. Right side always evaluates before the left side stores.

exercises.title: What does this print? (three variables)
tasks: Three variables exist. Only one changes. What does the last line print?
exercises.hint: Only score changes. Does that affect lives?
exercises.explanation: score changed but lives was never touched. Each variable is its own container. Changing one never affects the others.

exercises.title: Declare your birth month
tasks: Declare a variable that stores your birth month as a number.
exercises.hint: January is 1, December is 12. A variable needs a keyword, a name, an equals sign, and a value.
exercises.explanation: A complete variable declaration has four parts: let, a name you choose, =, and a value. Your birth month is a number so no quotes needed.

exercises.title: Run your first variable
tasks: Follow the code step by step. What does the console print?
exercises.hint: Read the right side of line 2 first: score + 5. What is score at that point?
exercises.explanation: score starts at 10. Then score + 5 = 15, and that gets stored back into score. So the console prints 15.

exercises.title: Drop the self-update
tasks: Fill in both blanks to add 3 to lives using lives itself.
exercises.hint: The variable name goes on both sides of the equals sign. Right side runs first.
exercises.explanation: lives = lives + 3 reads the current value of lives (2), adds 3, and stores the result (5) back. The variable name appears on both sides.

exercises.title: Type the full self-update
tasks: Add 5 to coins using coins itself.
exercises.hint: The variable name goes on the right side too. Read the right side first, then store the result.
exercises.explanation: coins = coins + 5 reads the current value (10), adds 5, and stores 15 back into coins. Right side always evaluates before the left side stores.

exercises.title: What does this print? (three variables)
tasks: Three variables exist. Only one changes. What does the last line print?
exercises.hint: Only score changes. Does that affect lives?
exercises.explanation: score changed but lives was never touched. Each variable is its own container. Changing one never affects the others.

exercises.title: Declare your birth month
tasks: Declare a variable that stores your birth month as a number.
exercises.hint: January is 1, December is 12. A variable needs a keyword, a name, an equals sign, and a value.
exercises.explanation: A complete variable declaration has four parts: let, a name you choose, =, and a value. Your birth month is a number so no quotes needed.

exercises.title: Run your first variable
tasks: Hit Run and look at the console.
See the number 0? That came from the variable score.
Change 0 to your age on line 1. Hit Run again.
You should see your age printed. You just used a variable!

steps.text: Hit Run and look at the console.

steps.title: Log your variable
tasks: A variable called score is already declared and set to 42.
Your job: write console.log(score); on the empty line.
Hit Run. You should see 42 in the console.
Now change score to 100 and run again. The console should show 100.
steps.hint: Write console.log(score); with no quotes around score. Quotes are for text. score is a variable name.

steps.title: Change the value
tasks: Score starts at 10. You'll see it printed first.
Your job: write one line between the two console.logs that changes score to 99.
No 'let' this time score already exists. Just write: score = 99;
Hit Run. You should see 10 first, then 99. The variable changed!
steps.hint: Just write score = 99; on the empty line. No 'let' that's only for brand new variables.

steps.title: Cookie math
tasks: You start with 12 cookies. Each step changes how many you have.
Step 1 is already done you ate 4 cookies.
Write Step 2: you found 6 more cookies. Add 6 to cookies.
Write Step 3: you shared them equally with a friend. Divide cookies by 2.
Hit Run and watch the console you should see the count change after each step.
steps.hint: To add 6: cookies = cookies + 6; To divide by 2: cookies = cookies / 2;

checks.name: cookies
checks.label: cookies ends at 7 after all three steps

steps.title: Write your own variable
tasks: This time the editor is mostly empty you'll write it yourself.
Declare a variable called favoriteNumber and set it to any number you like.
On the next line, log it: console.log(favoriteNumber);
Run it. See your number appear in the console.
Bonus: add a second variable called doubled and set it to favoriteNumber * 2. Log that too.
steps.hint: Start with: let favoriteNumber = 7; Then: console.log(favoriteNumber);

checks.label: something is printed to the console

challenges.title: Why is total always 0?
challenges.description: This code is supposed to add price to total, but total always prints 0. Find the bug.
challenges.hint: JavaScript is case-sensitive. Is the variable spelled exactly the same on every line?
challenges.explanation: JavaScript treats 'total' and 'Total' as completely different variables. Line 3 creates a brand new variable called 'Total' and never touches the original 'total' so total stays 0. Fix: change Total to total on line 3.

challenges.title: The message says 'Hello undefined' why?
challenges.description: The greeting should say 'Hello Sam' but something is undefined. Find and fix it.
challenges.hint: Look very carefully at how firstName is spelled on line 1 versus line 2.
challenges.explanation: The variable was declared as firstName (capital N) but used as firstname (lowercase n). JavaScript can't find 'firstname', so it uses undefined. Fix: change firstname to firstName on line 2.

challenges.title: Score should be 30 but it prints 15
challenges.description: There are two bugs here. The final score should be 30.
challenges.hint: How many times should you use the word 'let' on the same variable?
challenges.explanation: You can only use 'let' once per variable. Using it twice on 'score' causes an error. Remove the second 'let' so line 2 becomes: score = 5; — but then 5 * 3 = 15, not 30. The second bug is that line 2 should not exist at all. Remove it entirely. Then score = 10, and 10 * 3 = 30.
